core_claim: Narrative-Driven Recommendation (NDR) claims that the richest signal of what a person wants to watch is not their structured interaction history or a genre tag, but a free-text narrative describing their current intent — mood, plot appetite, comparative anchors ('like Shutter Island'), and situational context. The 2024 paper 'Large Language Models as Narrative-Driven Recommenders' (Eberhard, Ruprechter, Helic) demonstrates empirically that LLMs can decode these unstructured narratives into relevant item sets far better than vector-similarity baselines: GPT-4o scored an F1@10 roughly 70% higher than a doc2vec baseline on a gold-standard Reddit r/MovieSuggestions dataset. The central methodological claim is that the matching problem — narrative-to-item — is now tractable end-to-end without an explicit intermediate taxonomy, because the LLM internalises the latent dimensions (tone, theme, character, comparison) that a controlled vocabulary would otherwise have to enumerate. For HelpME2C this is the most directly relevant framework in the survey: it IS the cross-medium use case (a person describing in words what they want, matched to a catalogue), and it validates that free-text intent is a usable input modality.
descriptive_vs_experiential: NDR is deliberately agnostic — it captures whatever the user chooses to express, which in practice spans BOTH the descriptive ('a thriller with a mind-bending story', 'a heist movie') and the experiential ('I just want to see a movie where the good guy kicks some ass', 'something cosy to fall asleep to'). This is its distinguishing feature versus the other frameworks in the survey: it does not commit to a single dimension. The narrative request is a free-text surface that mixes topical content, affective target, mood-regulation intent, and comparative anchoring in one utterance. The LLM-based recommender does not separate these axes; it consumes the blended utterance and emits items. So the framework occupies a meta-level: it is not itself a taxonomy of narrative appeal, it is a MATCHING METHOD that can sit on top of any taxonomy (or none). The descriptive/experiential split is something the framework absorbs rather than resolves.
key_dimensions: NDR itself proposes no fixed axis set; that is the point. But the empirical work surfaces the recurring components of a real narrative request, which are the candidate dimensions: (1) Comparative anchor / seed items — 'like Inception', positively and negatively mentioned films (the Reddit dataset explicitly tracks positively-mentioned and negatively-mentioned movies per post); (2) Tone/mood — 'mind-bending', 'feel-good', 'dark'; (3) Plot/theme keywords — free-text descriptors of subject matter; (4) Genre constraints — when the user volunteers them; (5) Situational/context constraints — recency, runtime, who they are watching with, viewing occasion (more prominent in the POINTREC point-of-interest variant where context of the request is first-class); (6) Hard constraints — temporal (year), format (valid title), exclusions. The 2023 'LLM-Augmented NDR' work (Mysore, McCallum et al.) treats the narrative query as something that can be SYNTHESISED from user-item interactions via few-shot prompting, implying its dimensions are recoverable from behaviour plus a generative model — i.e. mood + theme + similar-to are the learnable structure.
cross_cultural_validity: Weak and untested. The flagship 2024 study is strictly single-language and single-community: English-language posts from one subreddit (r/MovieSuggestions), 296 test submissions from Nov 2016–Jul 2017. The authors explicitly flag single-domain/single-language as a limitation and suggest books/music as future work; cross-cultural and cross-lingual validity is simply not investigated. The matching engine (the LLM) carries whatever cultural priors its training data encoded — and the documented recency bias (strong preference for post-2000 films) is itself a cultural/temporal skew. For a narrative request written in a non-English language, or referencing non-Western comparative anchors (e.g. anime titles, regional cinema), there is no evidence either way. Given HelpME2C's anime + cross-medium scope, this is a genuine gap: the framework's method may transfer but its empirical grounding does not, and the comparative-anchor mechanism degrades if the catalogue or the user's reference points sit outside the LLM's well-covered (Western, recent, English) distribution. [uncertain]
empirical_grounding: Strong for the matching CLAIM, narrow in scope. The 2024 paper is a genuine large-scale computational evaluation: 38 open- and closed-source LLMs (LLama 3.2 through GPT-4o) against a gold-standard, crowdworker-annotated dataset, measured with Precision@10, Recall@10, F1@10, NDCG@10, plus inter-list diversity (Jaccard) and format-compliance checks. The headline result — LLMs beat doc2vec, GPT-4o ~70% higher F1 — is robust and the comparison to a real baseline is fair. The 2023 augmentation paper adds that retrieval models trained on LLM-synthesised narrative queries outperform retrieval and LLM baselines, a second independent empirical signal. What it does NOT establish: that the recommendations actually drive enjoyment, completion, or joint satisfaction — the ground truth is 'what did Reddit commenters suggest', a proxy for relevance, not measured downstream watch behaviour. To validate LLM-extracted theme dimensions against this framework you would need: (a) a held-out set of narrative requests with human relevance judgements (the Reddit dataset provides exactly this template), and (b) ideally downstream watch/completion data to close the loop from 'relevant suggestion' to 'actually enjoyed' — which no NDR paper yet has.
operationalisability: Highly operationalisable — this is the framework's strongest property and its direct relevance to HelpME2C. The extraction/matching unit is the WHOLE narrative utterance mapped to whole items; the LLM does the decomposition implicitly, so there is no brittle hand-built parser. Zero-shot prompting was found to perform on par with few-shot and identity prompting across model sizes, meaning the simplest possible integration works — you do not need exemplar libraries or persona engineering. Caveats / failure modes: (1) Recency bias — LLMs over-recommend post-2000 titles, so a synopsis-driven extractor will under-surface older catalogue items unless explicitly counterbalanced; (2) Small-model brittleness — tiny models produced only ~40% well-formed exact-10 outputs, so format compliance must be validated; (3) Title hallucination — the matching step needs exact/soft title resolution (the paper used Ratcliff/Obershelp soft matching at 0.9), because LLMs invent or misname items; (4) Catalogue grounding — an ungrounded LLM recommends from its parametric memory, not your catalogue, so HelpME2C must constrain output to the actual TMDB/AniList catalogue (retrieval-augmented, not free generation). For HelpME2C's synopsis-to-theme path specifically: the framework supports extracting theme slugs from a synopsis (it is the inverse direction, item-to-narrative), and the 2023 paper's query-synthesis result is direct evidence this works.
motivation_served: NDR is motivation-neutral by construction — it serves whatever motivation the user articulates in the narrative. In practice it captures hedonic requests ('fun, escapist, good-guy-wins'), eudaimonic ones ('something that will stay with me'), and mood-regulation ones ('something light, I've had a hard week') equally, because all three are expressible as free text. This is an advantage for group viewing: in a two-person decision, each person's narrative request can be elicited as text and the matching engine can search for items satisfying BOTH narratives (intersection) or trading off (the 2024 paper's inter-list diversity metric hints at producing varied candidate sets that could span two people's stated wants). The framework does not itself solve the trade-off arbitration — it provides the matching substrate on which a group-reconciliation layer (HelpME2C's ghost-profile / joint-want inference) would sit. The natural HelpME2C application: take two free-text 'what are you in the mood for' inputs, extract shared theme/tone dimensions, and rank catalogue items by joint fit.
receiver_dependence: NDR sits squarely on the RELATIONAL side, more than any other framework in the survey. The narrative request is, by definition, a property of a specific viewer at a specific moment ('I want X right now'), not a property of the work. The matching is interaction: work-features X this-user's-stated-intent. Some sub-components are absolute work properties the request references (a film IS or ISN'T a heist movie; gore level is fixed), but the relevance judgement is entirely receiver- and moment-dependent — the same film is a great match for 'mind-bending thriller' and a terrible match for 'something cosy'. For a two-person recommender this is the key structural fact: NDR gives you a clean separation between absolute item descriptors (extractable once from synopsis — genre, theme slugs, tone, content level) and the relational query (each viewer's transient intent). HelpME2C should encode the former as catalogue metadata (the controlled vocabulary) and treat the latter as per-session free-text input matched against it. The framework validates exactly this two-layer split.
blind_spots: (1) No theory of WHY a narrative maps to enjoyment — it is a matching method, not an explanation; it cannot tell you which theme dimensions are durable predictors of satisfaction versus noise. (2) Relevance != enjoyment — ground truth is 'suggested by a human commenter', so it optimises plausibility of suggestion, not measured watch/completion. (3) Recency and popularity bias bake in toward mainstream recent Western films, structurally disadvantaging long-tail, older, and non-English catalogue (acute for anime cross-medium). (4) Opacity — the LLM's implicit decomposition is uninspectable, so you cannot audit or correct the latent dimensions it uses; a controlled vocabulary is exactly the thing it dispenses with, which is a liability when you need explainability or want to guarantee coverage of specific themes. (5) Single-utterance assumption — it models a one-shot request well but not evolving/negotiated intent, which is what group viewing actually is. (6) Cold catalogue items — without grounding it cannot recommend items it has no parametric knowledge of (new releases, obscure titles).
vocabulary_implications: NDR's lesson for the vocabulary is INVERSION: rather than building the controlled vocabulary primarily as a matching index, treat it as the inspectable, auditable layer that an LLM-based matcher uses to ground its output to the real catalogue. Concrete implications: (1) The existing theme slugs are well-suited as the ITEM-SIDE descriptors extracted from synopses — NDR's 2023 query-synthesis result is direct evidence that mood/theme/similar-to structure is recoverable by an LLM, so synopsis-to-slug extraction is sound. (2) ADD explicit TONE/MOOD slugs as a first-class axis if not already present — narrative requests are saturated with affective descriptors (mind-bending, feel-good, cosy, bleak, tense) and the existing vocabulary's 'Texture' category (slow-burn, dark-comedy, absurdist-comedy) covers some but not the core mood register; propose adding mood slugs such as cosy-comfort, tense-and-gripping, feel-good-uplift, bleak-and-bracing, mind-bending. (3) ADD a COMPARATIVE-ANCHOR capability — not a slug but a mechanism: 'like <title>' is the single most common narrative component, so the system must support item-to-item similarity over the vocabulary, not just slug filtering. (4) NOT extractable from synopsis alone, requires different data: situational/occasion context (who is watching, time available, mood-of-the-day) — these are SESSION inputs, not catalogue metadata, and must be elicited per-request from the user(s), never inferred from the synopsis. (5) Add a CONTENT-LEVEL absolute axis (gore/violence/sexual-content intensity) — narrative requests frequently include hard exclusions, and these are absolute work properties that belong in the controlled vocabulary as filterable fields rather than themes.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: NDR validates the existing vocabulary as the ITEM-side descriptor layer but reveals it is incomplete on the QUERY-side affective register. Validated: the plot/theme/structure slugs (mystery-investigation, whodunit, heist-and-caper, time-loop, post-apocalyptic-survival, etc.) map cleanly onto the topical content of real narrative requests and are exactly the kind of thing the 2023 paper shows LLMs can synthesise from behaviour — these are sound. The Texture category (slow-burn, nonlinear-storytelling, dark-comedy, character-study, meditation-on-time/death) is the closest existing analogue to the experiential/mood component NDR emphasises, and is partially validated. Gaps revealed: (1) No dedicated MOOD/TONE axis — narrative requests lead with affect ('cosy', 'mind-bending', 'feel-good') and the current vocabulary forces these into Texture or genre, under-serving the most common request component; propose a distinct mood facet. (2) No COMPARATIVE-SIMILARITY mechanism — 'like X' is unrepresentable as a slug and needs item-embedding similarity alongside the controlled vocabulary. (3) No CONTENT-INTENSITY / hard-constraint fields — exclusions (no gore, no sad endings) are common in narratives and absent from the theme list. Misaligned: nothing is strongly contradicted; the relationship/emotion and moral/identity slugs (found-family, redemption-arc, ethical-dilemma) are richer than what most short narrative requests volunteer, so they are useful for item description but will be under-specified by terse user queries — implying the matcher must do query EXPANSION, not just literal matching.
key_sources: PRIMARY (free): Eberhard, Ruprechter, Helic (2024), 'Large Language Models as Narrative-Driven Recommenders', arXiv:2410.13604 — abstract https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.13604 , full HTML https://arxiv.org/html/2410.13604v1 , OpenReview https://openreview.net/forum?id=Bgr4RHYONd (all open access). Dataset: crowdworker-annotated r/MovieSuggestions gold standard. RELATED (free): Mysore, McCallum et al. (2023), 'Large Language Model Augmented Narrative Driven Recommendations', RecSys '17 lineage, arXiv:2306.02250 https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.02250 , ACM https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3604915.3608829 (ACM paywalled, arXiv free). FOUNDATIONAL: Bogers & Koolen (2017), 'Defining and Supporting Narrative-driven Recommendation', RecSys '17, https://dx.doi.org/10.1145/3109859.3109893 (ACM paywalled; ResearchGate copy https://www.researchgate.net/publication/319285421 ). CONTEXT variant: POINTREC test collection for narrative-driven POI recommendation, SIGIR '21, https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3404835.3463243 (paywalled).
